episode_title: Tav Klitgaard: How Zetland turned a newsroom problem into a global AI business

codes.description: Good Tape originated from Zetland's own newsroom need for Danish-language transcription, not from market analysis. The CEO was his own first user and would-be buyer.
excerpts: it really started with a need that I think many news organizations haven't had, which was we were spending so much time. You know, our journalists were spending so much time each week basically being robots sitting there with headphones on and typing into their keyboard
it was actually my as the CEO have said that it was my user need, I really needed this product for the newsroom
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: A developer discovered OpenAI's Whisper model via a GitHub commit just hours after it was published, sparking the entire Good Tape product. The open-source nature enabled distribution and scaling.
excerpts: one of our developers who was working on the news product, he discovers a commit on on GitHub. Like just a few hours after it it happened and and that was that was openai come whisper
I realized that this is an open source model. We can actually distribute this. We can actually put it on our own servers and distribute it to other people who have the same need
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: The transformative moment when a journalist experienced Whisper-based transcription for the first time and burst into the office demanding all resources be allocated to the project.
excerpts: the door like slams open and in comes this journalist like running, yelling, you know, stop everything, allocate all resources to this project that Jacob is working on because it's like it's magic. It works
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Danish as a small language meant existing transcription tools were 'totally useless,' creating both the pain point and the market opportunity that larger-language markets might have overlooked.
excerpts: Danish is a very small language and nobody was investing in Danish. So it was not only that it didn't really work. It sort of like didn't work at all. It was like totally useless what was out there in Danish
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland set out three to four specific hypotheses to validate before scaling Good Tape: are others struggling too, does international demand exist, and is there willingness to pay.
excerpts: we set out some hypotheses. And I believe we had like three or four hypotheses that we wanted to prove. And the first was are we the only one who are struggling with automating transcriptions?
the second one was, is there actually a need outside of Denmark? Like in the very beginning we were like we had to ask, you know, do people in Italy even transcribe?
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland deliberately chose to sell Good Tape as a B2C product despite enterprise users, banking on bottom-up adoption: five people in a newsroom using it would eventually get the boss to pay.
excerpts: we really figured out that we don't know how to sell B2B products. We are a newspaper, we sell to private consumers. Let's try selling this as if it were a B2C product
if five people in the same newsroom they're using the same transcription tool, well chances are that they will ask their boss to put on a credit card
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Instead of traditional sales, the product strategy centered on getting users to the 'wow moment' in seconds: just upload an audio file and drop your jaw at the quality.
excerpts: this is all about getting as fast as possible to the wow moment. And what we were able to do was to just get people to the wow moment in a matter of seconds
Just get them on the front page, make them find an audiophile on their computer. And just let them try it because people will drop their jaw when they actually experience the quality
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Good Tape was built for journalists but the market turned out to be far larger: researchers, students, business consultants, psychologists, and other professionals who transcribe.
excerpts: we thought this was a product for journalists because that was the need we knew from ourselves but very very quickly we we figured out yeah, but there's also like, you know, researchers, students, business consultants, even like psychologists
the market is much bigger than we thought
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: The journalism brand became a competitive advantage: non-journalist users trusted a tool built for journalists because journalists are associated with high standards for interviewing and data protection.
excerpts: I just want to use whatever tool the journalists are using because I know that journalists they are the ones who are good at interviewing
if it's good enough for journalists, it's definitely good enough for me
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: For Zetland, data security goes beyond GDPR compliance to journalistic source protection, which became a meaningful product differentiator in a market where such standards are not universal.
excerpts: for us it's not just GDPR, yeah of course, but that's table stakes. It's also like really source protections. It cannot leak. We have to have the highest the highest standards of data protection
that's not a given in all industries. And I think people know that
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland demonstrated that a newsroom can build, monetize, and successfully divest an AI product, challenging the assumption that media companies cannot be technology companies.
excerpts: it's actually been a profitable company for like it almost it's like it almost instantly was a profitable company. We spun it out from the get-go because we knew it had to be its own thing
at one point it was almost like three million dollars that a good tape was making
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Engineering philosophy: if improving transcription quality by one percent requires an extra minute or two of processing, that trade-off is always worth making. Quality is always the most important metric.
excerpts: quality is the most important always. Like if we can improve the transcription quality by one percent, you know, it doesn't really matter if it takes a minute longer or two minutes longer to process
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: The competitive advantage of Good Tape lies not in the model itself but in the workflow optimization around it: pre-processing audio before it enters the model and post-processing output afterward.
excerpts: the secret source behind the quality of good tape is in many ways what happens before the audio file goes into the model and what happens afterwards
nowadays the technology is pretty advanced. It's actually sometimes not so much, you know, training the models. It's more like optimizing the workflows around the models
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Building Good Tape right before ChatGPT launched gave Zetland accidental early AI literacy; staff were discussing hallucinations and AI models over lunch while the rest of the industry was just discovering them.
excerpts: we launched the day before the first version of ChatGPT. And that meant that already when the whole hype was building around ChatGPT, we were working with it
there was a lot of organizational learning that actually happened very fast, sort of like by coincidence, because we were like just like talking over about these things over lunch every day
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Working with AI practically from early on removed the fear that AI was 'out for people's jobs'; making robots do the robotic work (transcription) framed AI as liberator rather than threat.
excerpts: it took away some of the the idea that there could have been like the that AI was something that was, you know, out for people's jobs or something. We've never had that feeling here

something like transcriptions, well that is kind of just a very obvious use case and this big evil thing called AI, well, it can solve that use case and it can make journalists lead happier lives because they don't have to spend their life being robots
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland is currently using AI for productivity gains but the transformative shift will come when AI moves from productivity to creativity, which they are approaching more slowly than others due to the nature of their product.
excerpts: we are still at a point where we're using AI for productivity gains. That is transcription is a good example, but there's a lot of other things that we're using it for

for me the big shift comes when we move from productivity to creativity. And we are of course in that move right now. We are probably moving a little bit slower than many other organizations because of the nature of our product
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland defines its product as fundamentally human: personal storytelling, journalist-audience connection, trust built through vulnerability and doubt. This identity constrains AI adoption for editorial output.
excerpts: Zetland's product is a human product. And that means and it's not about speed. It's really about quality. And it's about being personal and telling stories in a personal way
I'm not in doubt that it probably will be good enough one day. But it's not there yet
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Philosophical concern that AI voices may soon sound more human than actual humans because machines can be engineered to sound human while humans 'just talk' naturally and imperfectly.
excerpts: what does it mean when the machine is actually more human than a human being because the machine knows what it means to sound like a human being. Oftentimes a human being doesn't know that. You know, we're just humans. Like we just talk. But a machine knows how to make it sound like it's really human
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: The journalist-audience trust relationship, built through personal and journalistic integrity, is framed as 'probably one of the last frontiers' that AI cannot replicate, but which erodes when voice cloning tricks audiences.
excerpts: we are there with our journalistic integrity and we are there with our human integrity and our personal integrity. And I think that is probably one of the last frontiers

when that trust erodes because of people basically tricking people to think that it's a different person talking. I can be a little bit worried about what that means for people's perception of humanity or of humanness
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Distinguishing between AI-generated content (utility, like design) and human-created journalism (intentional, like art) through the analogy that art's value lies in human intention, not the artifact itself.
excerpts: it's the same as thinking about the difference between you know, art and design. Design is something that you use it for something. And art might be just a white canvas with two lines on it
the value lies in the attention, the human intention from the artist
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Even if a human-recorded and AI-generated audio waveform were identical, knowing it was made by a real human would make it more valuable, suggesting provenance matters more than quality.
excerpts: let's say hypothetically that the actual sound file you know, of this recorded conversation was waveform was exactly the same, but in one instance I knew that it was generated and in the other instance I knew that it was not generated. The value of the letter would be much, much higher to me
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland's storytelling approach uses journalistic doubt, mistakes, and the editorial process itself as trust-building tools, paralleling how TikTok creators build authenticity through imperfection.
excerpts: just getting to know the personalities and the values and you know to be honest also the mistakes and the doubts and I mean that's what we try and use a lot in our storytelling
we want to be there in the process when the journalists kind of decide, okay, what did that source say? But and it didn't really match what she said
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: An audio producer was already using AI voice cloning to correct journalist recordings, violating guidelines, but the use case turned out to be sensible; this led to updating principles rather than punishment.
excerpts: the audio producer said, well, I'm already doing that. And Thomas and me were like, oh, oh what?

it was just an example of us actually, you know, leaning into that this guy had just done it because he thought that was the right thing to do and then afterward we could actually agree, yeah. I mean that was against our guidelines, but it was actually probably the right thing to do. So let's change our guidelines
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Using 'principles' rather than 'guidelines' because principles about trust are durable while tool-specific guidelines become obsolete with each new technology release.
excerpts: we actually also don't call them guidelines, we call them principles because guidelines will change all the time. It'll change every time a new service comes up every time something new becomes possible
there are some principles that we will always adhere to. That all has to do with building trust
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: AI principles should be open enough to not stop experimentation: 'you cannot trick people' is better than 'you cannot use ChatGPT' because the latter blocks exploration.
excerpts: the principles or the guidelines that of course should be there and in place and should be communicated and should be discussed, they need to be open enough for people to not stop experimenting
if it says in a guideline you cannot use chat GPT. I mean I hope that doesn't. But then it's like okay well then I'm not going to experiment with that
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Explicit decision not to clone Danish journalist voices for other language markets despite AI translation capability, because a cloned voice cannot authentically be 'a friend in your kitchen.'
excerpts: we have no intentions of cloning our journalists' voice and translating that using an AI and then using the AI voice in another country
it basically doesn't really fit with the idea of the journalist being a good friend sitting in your kitchen and telling you a story. That has to be like a real person that you can get to know
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Audio-first distribution has proven particularly appealing to younger audiences, with 45% of paying members in their twenties or thirties, connecting to a generation that prefers listening over reading.
excerpts: we do forty five percent of our paying members there in their twenties or thirties
we can see just that it's even more popular among the younger parts of our audience than it is with the older
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland's journalistic approach mirrors what works on social platforms: personal, conversational, 'like a friend in your kitchen,' as opposed to buttoned-up traditional news delivery.
excerpts: we want the stories to be told like if it was a friend who sat next to you in your kitchen and told you a story about, you know, this thing is happening with the housing prices
a not so buttoned up way of telling things
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland does not have an 'AI strategy' but has an innovation strategy; AI is treated like a personal computer -- obviously used, but not elevated to a strategic category of its own.
excerpts: we don't have an AI strategy as such, but we definitely have an innovation strategy. I mean to be honest to me, AI is sort of like it's it's like talking about a personal computer. It's like, yeah, of course we're using that
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: AI-generated podcasts (e.g., NotebookLM) are useful for processing information faster but do not create human connection; they are utility, not relationship.
excerpts: when I listen to podcasts generated by a notebook. And I do that a lot, but it's not I don't have any human connections to the hosts. So for me that's a utility. It's convenient. It makes it so that I can process information faster
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Personal shift from Google to AI tools (Gemini, Perplexity) for information retrieval, described as a 'very big change' that provides a 'much, much better experience' without ads.
excerpts: just remembering like just three months back I would Google things. I never Google things anymore. And that's just a very big change actually in my private life
it's just a very different and a much, much, much, much, much, much better experience that I get now from using whatever I use, Gemini or Perplexity
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: AI search replacing Google reduces ad exposure; as a CEO who hates ads, recognizes this is great for users but existentially threatening to ad-supported news business models.
excerpts: just remembering six months back. I feel like there were so many ads that I was seeing all the time and I hate ads
leads us to the the problem of business models and we could have had an entire episode probably about on this show about what does this mean for the newsroom business models
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Observation that the news industry is sometimes too humble about its strengths, particularly its high-quality data, rigorous standards, and brand trust that tech companies lack.
excerpts: I think sometimes we also as an industry look at ourselves like I think sometimes we're too humble. We're working really with like high quality
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland spun out Good Tape, grew it to profitability, then divested it as part of a larger transaction, demonstrating a viable build-monetize-exit model for newsroom-built AI products.
excerpts: we recently as a part of of a bigger transaction deal in Zetland, we divested the company. And actually got a very nice return on the investment and the risk that we took in on it
now it's up to someone else to make it grow even bigger
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland's relatively large product team and agile culture allowed rapid resource reallocation to Good Tape when the opportunity emerged, demonstrating organizational flexibility.
excerpts: we're a fairly small company, but it's relatively big, and that also meant that we could allocate resources. We could kind of we work very agile and we could say, okay, this might be a very interesting product. Let's take whatever resources out for whatever months and let's start building that
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

codes.description: Zetland's strategy to scale impact by expanding its model into new countries (Finland, potentially more), replicating the product but with local human journalists, not AI translations.
excerpts: Denmark is a very small market, it's a very small country. The impact that we can get here is big in Danish standards, but we wanna kind of grow our impact and that means scaling the product
every time we need we go into a new territory, we need to kind of replicate the product. We need to basically start from scratch
codes.speaker: Tav Klitgaard

episode_summary: This episode traces Zetland's journey from discovering an OpenAI Whisper commit on GitHub to building Good Tape into a profitable, globally adopted transcription tool that was eventually divested, providing a rare case study of a newsroom successfully productizing an AI solution and generating meaningful revenue from it. The deeper analytical thread concerns Zetland's deliberate tension between embracing AI pragmatically (for productivity, transcription, internal tools) and protecting what they call their 'human product': audio-first journalism built on personal connection, journalist vulnerability, and audience trust. Klitgaard's most philosophically provocative contribution is the idea that AI may soon sound 'more human than human' because machines can be engineered for naturalness while humans simply talk, raising unresolved questions about what provenance and authenticity mean when the artifact becomes indistinguishable. The episode also reveals how accidental early exposure to AI through building Good Tape defanged organizational fear of AI, and how a voice-cloning guidelines violation led not to punishment but to principle refinement, illustrating a 'principles over guidelines' approach to AI governance.
surprises: Zetland does not have an 'AI strategy' despite being one of the most successful newsroom AI product creators; they frame AI as simply an obvious tool within a broader innovation strategy.

An audio producer was already cloning journalist voices to correct small mistakes before leadership knew, and the response was to update principles rather than discipline, revealing a genuinely experimental culture.
The philosophical claim that AI voices may become 'more human than human' because machines know what human-sounding means while actual humans do not, inverting the usual framing of AI as artificial.

Good Tape's B2C go-to-market strategy for what is essentially a B2B product, born from honest admission that 'we don't know how to sell B2B products,' proved more effective than traditional enterprise sales.
tensions: Building and profiting from AI tools (Good Tape) while maintaining that Zetland's core product must remain fundamentally human and resistant to AI-generated editorial content.
Refusing to clone journalist voices for international expansion while having an audio producer who was already cloning voices for corrections, revealing the blurriness of AI voice boundaries.
Enthusiasm for AI search as 'much better' user experience vs. acknowledgment that this shift destroys the ad-supported business models that most of the industry depends on.

Defining the product as 'human' while acknowledging that 'I'm not in doubt that it probably will be good enough one day,' suggesting the human-product identity is a temporal differentiator, not a permanent one.
Principles-over-guidelines governance enabling experimentation but also allowing unanticipated AI use (voice cloning) that violates existing rules before leadership even knows about it.
